# Transformers in Practice: Applications

## Outline

-   How the three transformer families are applied to core NLP **downstream tasks**:
    -   Text classification
    -   Question answering
    -   Sequence-to-sequence tasks (translation, summarization)
    -   Open-ended generation (chat, code generation)



---

## From Pre-training to Downstream Tasks

Pre-training gives the model general language knowledge.
**Fine-tuning** adapts it to a specific task using a labelled dataset.


```{mermaid}
graph TB
    A["Large unlabelled corpus"] --> B["Pre-trained model"]
    B --> C["Fine-tune on task data"]
    C --> D["Task-specific model"]
```



::: {.callout-tip}
**language knowledge transfers**:  fine-tuning needs far less data than training from scratch.
:::


## Text Classification

**Architecture:** Encoder-only (BERT, RoBERTa)



Given a sequence $w_{1:n}$, prepend a special `[CLS]` token; use its final hidden state $h_{\text{[CLS]}} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ as the sentence representation:

::: {style="width: 60%; margin: auto;"}
![Transformer encoder architecture showing the [CLS] token prepended to the input sequence, with its final hidden state $h_{[CLS]}$ extracted as the sentence representation for downstream classification tasks](../images/transformer-cls.png)
:::

A linear classification head maps $h_{\text{[CLS]}}$ to class probabilities:

$$
\hat{y} = \text{softmax}(W \, h_{\text{[CLS]}} + b)
$$

where $W \in \mathbb{R}^{C \times d}$ and $C$ is the number of classes.


---

## Regime 1 — End-to-End Fine-Tuning (standard BERT)
 
The classifier and the Transformer are trained **jointly** via a single backpropagation pass:
 
$$\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(\hat{y}, y)
  \xrightarrow{\text{backprop}} W, b \;\text{ AND }\; \theta_{\text{Transformer}}$$
 
- $h_{\texttt{[CLS]}}$ **evolves** during training.
- The Transformer learns representations that are **adapted to the classification task**.
- The two components are **tightly coupled**.
 
---
 
## Regime 2 — Feature Extraction (frozen encoder)
 
The pre-trained Transformer is **frozen**; only the classifier head is trained.
 
| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Extract $h_{\texttt{[CLS]}}$ as a **fixed** feature vector |
| 2 | Train $W, b$ supervised on the downstream task |
 
- No gradient flows into $\theta_{\text{Transformer}}$.
- The two stages are **fully decoupled**.
- Computationally cheaper, but less flexible.
 
---
 

**Typical tasks:**

| Task | Example |
|---|---|
| Sentiment analysis | positive / negative / neutral |
| Topic classification | sports, politics, science… |
| Spam detection | spam / ham |
| Intent detection | book_flight, check_balance… |

::: {.callout-note}
Fine-tuning BERT on sentiment analysis (SST-2) takes a few minutes on a GPU and reaches $\approx 93\%$ accuracy, vs. $\approx 85\%$ for a classical SVM on TF-IDF features.
:::

## Question Answering (Extractive QA)

**Architecture:** Encoder-only (BERT-style)

Input: `[CLS] question [SEP] context [SEP]`

Two linear heads predict the **start** and **end** positions of the answer span in the context:

$$
s_t = w_{\text{start}}^T h_t, \qquad e_t = w_{\text{end}}^T h_t
$$

The answer is the span $[i^*, j^*]$ that maximises $s_{i^*} + e_{j^*}$ with $j^* \geq i^*$.
where $i^*$ and $j^*$ are considered as semantic scores for the start and end of the answer span, respectively. 

They are high when the corresponding token positions are likely to be the start and end of the answer in the context. 

The model learns to assign high scores to the correct start and end positions during training, allowing it to extract the relevant span from the context when given a question.





---


## Example: BERT on SQuAD 1.1
 
This example is drawn from the **Prime number** article in SQuAD 1.1 (dev set).
 
> *"The property of being prime is called primality. A simple but slow method of verifying
> the primality of a given number $n$ is known as **trial division**."*
 
**Question:** What is the name of the method of verifying the primality of a given number?
 
**Gold answer:** `trial division`
 
---
 
## Token-level scores (toy illustration)
 
After fine-tuning, BERT produces a start score $s_t$ and an end score $e_t$ for each token
via two learned projection vectors $w_{\text{start}}, w_{\text{end}} \in \mathbb{R}^H$ :
 
$$s_t = w_{\text{start}}^\top h_t, \qquad e_t = w_{\text{end}}^\top h_t.$$
 
| $t$ | Token | $s_t$ (start) | $e_t$ (end) | notes |
|-----|-------|--------------|------------|-------|
| 1  | The | −2.1 | −1.8 | article |
| 2  | property | −1.4 | −1.2 | |
| 3  | of | −1.9 | −1.7 | |
| 4  | being | −1.3 | −1.1 | |
| 5  | prime | −0.8 | −0.6 | |
| 6  | is | −1.5 | −1.3 | |
| 7  | called | −1.2 | −1.0 | |
| 8  | primality | −0.4 | −0.2 | plausible but wrong |
| 9  | . | −2.3 | −2.1 | punctuation |
| 10 | A | −1.8 | −1.6 | |
| 11 | simple | −0.3 | −0.1 | |
| 12 | but | −1.6 | −1.4 | |
| 13 | slow | −0.5 | −0.3 | |
| 14 | method | −0.2 | +0.1 | |
| 15 | of | −1.7 | −1.5 | |
| 16 | verifying | −0.6 | −0.4 | |
| 17 | the | −1.9 | −1.7 | |
| 18 | primality | −0.7 | −0.5 | |
| 19 | of | −1.8 | −1.6 | |
| 20 | a | −1.7 | −1.5 | |
| 21 | given | −0.9 | −0.7 | |
| 22 | number | −1.1 | −0.9 | |
| 23 | is | −1.4 | −1.2 | |
| 24 | known | −1.0 | −0.8 | |
| 25 | as | −1.3 | −1.1 | |
| 26 | **trial** | **+3.8** | +0.2 | ← best start |
| 27 | **division** | +0.4 | **+4.1** | ← best end |
| 28 | . | −2.2 | −2.0 | punctuation |
 
---
 
## Span selection
 
The predicted span maximises $f(i,j) = s_i + e_j$ subject to $j \geq i$ :
 
$$f(26,\,27) = 3.8 + 4.1 = \mathbf{7.9} \quad \leftarrow \text{global maximum}$$
 
A few competing pairs for comparison :
 
| $(i, j)$ | span | $s_i + e_j$ |
|----------|------|------------|
| (26, 27) | *trial division* | **7.9** ✓ |
| (8, 8) | *primality* | $-0.4 + (-0.2) = -0.6$ |
| (11, 14) | *simple but slow method* | $-0.3 + 0.1 = -0.2$ |
| (26, 28) | *trial division .* | $3.8 + (-2.0) = 1.8$ |
| (1, 27) | entire sentence fragment | $-2.1 + 4.1 = 2.0$ |
 
The constraint $j \geq i$ eliminates impossible spans (end before start),
but it does **not** favour long spans: the longest candidate $(1,\,27)$ scores only $2.0$,
far below the correct short span $(26,\,27)$.
 



## Results on SQuAD 1.1
 
| System | EM | F1 |
|--------|----|----|
| Human performance | 82.3 | **91.2** |
| BERT Large (single model) | 84.1 | **93.2** |
| BERT Large (ensemble) | 85.8 | **93.6** |
 
BERT Large achieves **F1 = 93.2** (single model), **above human performance (91.2)** by +2.0 points.
 
> "For the first time, a machine surpasses human-level performance on SQuAD 1.1."
> — Devlin et al. (2018)
 
---
 


## Sequence-to-Sequence Tasks

**Architecture:** Encoder-Decoder (T5, BART, mBART)

These tasks require generating a full output sequence, not just a label or span.

| Task | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Machine translation | "I love Paris" | "J'aime Paris" |
| Summarization | long article | short abstract |
| Text simplification | complex sentence | simpler sentence |
| Grammar correction | sentence with errors | corrected sentence |

The training loss is the standard conditional language model loss:

$$
L = - \sum_t \log P(v_t \mid v_{1:t-1},\, w_{1:n})
$$

::: {.callout-tip}
T5 ("Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer") frames **every NLP task** as text-to-text: even classification becomes generating the string `"positive"` or `"negative"`.
:::

## Text Generation (Decoder-only)

**Architecture:** Decoder-only (GPT family, LLaMA, Mistral)

Given a prompt, the model generates tokens autoregressively:

$$
P(w_{1:T}) = \prod_{t=1}^T P(w_t \mid w_{1:t-1})
$$

Typical applications:

-   **Chatbots / assistants** (ChatGPT, Claude)
-   **Code generation** (GitHub Copilot, CodeLlama)
-   **Creative writing** and content generation
-   **Instruction following** (after SFT + RLHF)

Generation quality depends heavily on the **decoding strategy** (greedy, beam search, top-$k$/top-$p$, temperature) seen in the previous chapter.



## Summary: Architecture × Task

| Task | Architecture | Loss at fine-tuning |
|---|---|---|
| Classification | Encoder (CLS head) | Cross-entropy (1 token) |
| Extractive QA | Encoder (span head) | Cross-entropy (start + end) |
| Translation, summarization | Encoder-Decoder | CLM on decoder output |
| Open-ended generation | Decoder-only | CLM |

---
